StoredPlayer.brief: Represents a node in a doubly linked list that stores information about a player.
StoredPlayer.details: The StoredPlayer class functions as a node within a doubly linked list structure, encapsulating key information related to a stored player. It contains member variables for the player's name, the identifier of the character who performed the storage action, and the timestamp of the last save or update. The class maintains pointers to the next and previous nodes, enabling efficient bidirectional traversal and modification of the list. It provides a default constructor for creating empty nodes and a virtual destructor to ensure proper cleanup, especially in inheritance scenarios. Copy construction and assignment are explicitly disabled to maintain the integrity of list nodes, preventing unintended copying. This class is integral to systems managing persistent player data, supporting operations such as insertion, deletion, lookup, and data persistence within the storage management framework.
StoredPlayer.notes: The class employs the singleton pattern for list management by linking nodes through 'next' and 'previous' pointers. Copy constructor and assignment operator are private to prevent copying, ensuring each node's uniqueness within the list.
StoredPlayer.rationale: StoredPlayer exists to serve as the fundamental data unit within the player storage system, enabling efficient management, traversal, and persistence of player data. Its design supports the system's need for reliable, bidirectional navigation of stored player records, facilitating operations like lookup, insertion, and removal in a linked list structure.
StoredPlayer.name: StoredPlayer

name.brief: Stores the player's name within the StoredPlayer data structure.
name.details: The 'name' variable holds the identifier for a player, utilizing the custom String class which extends std::string with additional features such as case-insensitive comparisons and advanced manipulation methods. It is initialized when reading data from files or during player creation, and is used extensively in functions that compare, insert, or remove player records based on their names. This variable facilitates case-insensitive operations, ensuring consistent handling of player identifiers across various components of the system.
name.notes: The String class's extended features, particularly case-insensitive comparison, are leveraged in operations involving 'name' to ensure reliable matching regardless of letter case.
name.rationale: This variable exists to uniquely identify players within the storage system, supporting operations like lookup, insertion, and removal by providing a consistent and case-insensitive reference to each player's name.
name.name: name

by_who.brief: Stores the name of the character responsible for performing the storage action.
by_who.details: The 'by_who' variable records the identifier of the character who executed the storage operation for a player. It is set when reading storage data from files and is used to identify and verify the actor responsible during save, load, or audit processes. The variable employs the String class, which offers advanced string manipulation features and case-insensitive comparisons, facilitating reliable identification regardless of input case variations. This field is essential for maintaining accurate records of storage actions and supporting accountability within the system.
by_who.notes: Utilizes the custom String class for flexible and case-insensitive handling of character names, ensuring consistency across storage and retrieval operations.
by_who.rationale: This variable exists to track which character performed the storage, enabling proper auditing, accountability, and contextual referencing of storage actions within the system.
by_who.name: by_who

date.brief: A string that records the timestamp of the last save or update of the player's data.
date.details: The 'date' variable stores a timestamp indicating when the player's data was last saved or modified. It is represented as a String object, which allows for flexible formatting and manipulation. This variable is typically initialized by reading from a save file or set during save operations, ensuring that each StoredPlayer instance accurately reflects its last update time. It is frequently accessed during file I/O functions to record or retrieve the last modification timestamp, supporting data consistency and version tracking.
date.notes: The 'date' is stored as a custom String class, enabling advanced string operations beyond standard strings. Proper formatting and parsing are important when reading from or writing to storage to maintain consistency.
date.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate tracking of when player data was last stored or modified, supporting features such as version control, data validation, and user information display regarding last save times.
date.name: date

usages.wiz__gen_8cc, void do_storage(Character: Records the timestamp of storage, formatted and stored in the 'date' field of StoredPlayer.",

usages.storage_8cc, void save_line(FILE *fp, St: Accessed to retrieve the date of storage for output via fprintf.

next.brief: Pointer to the next node in the linked list of stored players.
next.details: The 'next' pointer in the StoredPlayer class links individual player records within a singly linked list structure. It is initialized to nullptr when a node is created, indicating the end of the list. This pointer is used extensively during list operations such as insertion, traversal, and removal, enabling sequential access to all stored player entries. Proper management of this pointer is essential for maintaining the integrity of the list during modifications. It facilitates efficient iteration over the collection of stored players, supporting functions that load, save, insert, or remove player data.
next.notes: Since 'next' is a raw pointer, careful handling is required to prevent memory leaks or dangling pointers during list modifications.
next.rationale: The 'next' pointer exists to connect individual StoredPlayer nodes, forming a linked list that allows dynamic management of stored player data, supporting operations like insertion, deletion, and traversal efficiently.
next.name: next

previous.brief: Pointer to the previous node in the doubly linked list of stored players.
previous.details: The 'previous' variable is a pointer that references the preceding StoredPlayer node within a doubly linked list structure used to manage stored player data. It is initialized to nullptr upon node creation, signifying that the node is either at the start of the list or not yet linked. This pointer plays a crucial role during list operations such as insertion, removal, and traversal by enabling bidirectional navigation and efficient updates of neighboring nodes' links. It works in conjunction with the 'next' pointer to maintain the list's integrity and facilitate dynamic modifications to the list structure.
previous.notes: The 'previous' pointer is typically set during insertion and removal operations to ensure proper bidirectional linkage. Proper management of this pointer is essential to prevent list corruption and dangling references.
previous.rationale: This variable exists to support efficient bidirectional traversal and modification of the stored players list, enabling operations like insertion and removal to be performed with minimal overhead and maintaining the structural integrity of the list.
previous.name: previous

StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().brief: Constructs an empty StoredPlayer instance with default-initialized members.
StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().details: This default constructor initializes a new StoredPlayer object by default-constructing all its member variables. It does not perform any additional setup or validation, providing a simple way to create an uninitialized or placeholder object that can be configured later. This is useful in scenarios where a StoredPlayer needs to be declared before being populated with data.
StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().returns: This constructor does not return a value.
StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().notes: Since the constructor is trivial, it does not perform any complex operations or throw exceptions. It relies on member default constructors, making it efficient for creating placeholder objects.
StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().rationale: This constructor is used when an instance of StoredPlayer needs to be created without immediate initialization, such as in container allocations or deferred setup scenarios.
StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer().name: StoredPlayer

virtual StoredPlayer::~StoredPlayer().brief: Virtual destructor for the StoredPlayer class to ensure proper cleanup of resources.
virtual StoredPlayer::~StoredPlayer().details: This destructor is declared as virtual to allow correct destruction of derived class instances through base class pointers. Although the base class destructor has an empty implementation, it serves as a crucial hook for derived classes to implement their own cleanup procedures, preventing resource leaks and ensuring proper object lifecycle management. The destructor's primary role is to facilitate resource deallocation when a StoredPlayer object is destroyed, either explicitly or when it goes out of scope.
virtual StoredPlayer::~StoredPlayer().notes: Having a virtual destructor is essential in polymorphic base classes to avoid undefined behavior during object destruction. This design supports safe inheritance and resource management in the class hierarchy.
virtual StoredPlayer::~StoredPlayer().rationale: This destructor is used when a StoredPlayer object is destroyed, either explicitly or automatically, to ensure that all associated resources are properly released, especially in inheritance scenarios where derived classes may need to perform additional cleanup.
virtual StoredPlayer::~StoredPlayer().name: ~StoredPlayer

StoredPlayer::StoredPlayer(const StoredP.details: This constructor creates a new instance of the StoredPlayer class by copying the state of an existing StoredPlayer object. It ensures that the new object is an exact duplicate of the original, including all relevant data and resources.

StoredPlayer & StoredPlayer::operator=(c.brief: Assigns the values from another StoredPlayer object to this one.
StoredPlayer & StoredPlayer::operator=(c.details: This assignment operator copies the data from the given StoredPlayer object to the current instance. It ensures that all relevant fields are duplicated, maintaining the integrity of the object's state. This operator handles self-assignment by checking if the source and destination are the same object.
